Questions.Title: Definition of Data Quality
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following best defines data quality?
Questions.Hint: Think about the relationship between a dataset and its intended use.
Questions.Feedback: Data quality refers to the degree to which a dataset is fit for its intended purpose in analysis, decision-making, or reporting. Quality is always evaluated relative to the use case.

Options.Text: The degree to which a dataset is fit for its intended purpose in analysis, decision-making, or reporting
The total number of rows and columns contained within a dataset
The speed at which data can be retrieved from a database system
The format in which data is stored across different file types

Questions.Title: Principle Behind Data Quality Importance
Questions.QuestionText: Which principle best captures why data quality matters in analysis?
Questions.Hint: Think of a well-known saying about input and output quality.
Questions.Feedback: The principle 'garbage in, garbage out' highlights that even sophisticated analytical techniques cannot compensate for fundamentally flawed data, making data quality essential to reliable analysis.

Options.Text: Garbage in, garbage out
Less is more
Occam's razor
The law of large numbers

Questions.Title: Accuracy Dimension of Data Quality
Questions.QuestionText: What does the accuracy dimension of data quality specifically measure?
Questions.Hint: Consider how closely recorded values match real-world values.
Questions.Feedback: Accuracy refers to how closely the values in a dataset reflect the true, real-world values they are intended to represent. Issues often arise from manual entry errors, sensor malfunctions, or faulty data transfer.

Options.Text: How closely dataset values reflect true, real-world values they represent
Whether all required data values are present in the dataset
Whether values are uniform and non-contradictory across records
The trustworthiness and stability of a data source over time

Questions.Title: Consistency Issue Example
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of a data consistency issue?
Questions.Hint: Think about the same entity being labeled differently across records.
Questions.Feedback: Consistency ensures data values are uniform and do not contradict each other. Labeling the same country as 'USA', 'U.S.A.', and 'United States' in different records is a classic consistency problem.

Options.Text: A product labeled 'USA', 'U.S.A.', and 'United States' in different records
A customer database missing date-of-birth values for 30% of records
A salary field containing a value of $1,000,000 among mostly $30,000–$80,000 values
A survey tool that randomly fails to record responses for some participants

Questions.Title: Reliability in Data Quality
Questions.QuestionText: What best describes the reliability dimension of data quality?
Questions.Hint: Think about the stability and trustworthiness of the data source itself.
Questions.Feedback: Reliability refers to the trustworthiness and stability of a data source — whether the data consistently produces the same values under the same conditions. Inconsistent methodology across time periods can undermine reliability.

Options.Text: The trustworthiness and stability of a data source producing consistent values under the same conditions
The percentage of records that contain no missing values in a dataset
The degree to which data values match their true real-world counterparts
The absence of duplicate records across integrated data systems

Questions.Title: Common Missing Value Representations
Questions.QuestionText: Which of the following is a common placeholder used to encode missing values that analysis tools may not automatically recognize?
Questions.Hint: Think about numeric or string stand-ins used when data is absent.
Questions.Feedback: Common placeholders for missing data include -999, 'N/A', 'unknown', or empty strings. These may not be automatically recognized as missing by analysis tools and must be explicitly identified.

Options.Text: FALSE

Questions.Title: MNAR Missingness Mechanism Defined
Questions.QuestionText: Which scenario best illustrates Missing Not At Random (MNAR)?
Questions.Hint: Think about whether the reason for missing data is related to the missing value itself.
Questions.Feedback: MNAR occurs when the reason data is missing is directly related to the missing value itself, such as high earners deliberately skipping income fields because of their high income.

Options.Text: High earners deliberately skip an income field because of their high earnings
A sensor randomly fails, causing some temperature readings to be absent
Older respondents skip income questions, but the pattern is explained by age
A data entry operator accidentally omits values for a random subset of records

Questions.Title: Threshold for Dropping Missing Columns
Questions.QuestionText: According to a common rule of thumb, at what missing data threshold should an analyst consider dropping a column rather than imputing it?
Questions.Hint: Think about the percentage range mentioned in the context of missing value detection.
Questions.Feedback: A common rule of thumb is that columns missing more than 40–50% of their values may need to be dropped rather than imputed, though this threshold depends on context and dataset size.

Options.Text: 40–50% of values missing
5–10% of values missing
60–70% of values missing
More than 90% of values missing

Questions.Title: Cause of Duplicate Data in Enterprises
Questions.QuestionText: What is identified as a frequent cause of duplicate data in enterprise environments?
Questions.Hint: Think about what happens when data from multiple sources is combined without proper checks.
Questions.Feedback: Data integration pipelines that merge multiple sources without deduplication logic are a frequent culprit of duplicate records in enterprise environments.

Options.Text: Data integration pipelines merging sources without deduplication logic
Analysts manually entering data into spreadsheets
Using outdated database software that lacks indexing features
Applying log transformation to right-skewed data distributions

Questions.Title: Python Tools for Detecting Duplicates
Questions.QuestionText: Which Python pandas methods are specifically mentioned for detecting and removing duplicate records?
Questions.Hint: Think about methods whose names directly reference duplication.
Questions.Feedback: Pandas provides the duplicated() method to detect duplicates and drop_duplicates() to remove them. SQL can use GROUP BY with HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 for detection.

Options.Text: duplicated() and drop_duplicates()
isnull() and dropna()
merge() and concat()
describe() and info()

Questions.Title: Z-Score Outlier Detection Suitability
Questions.QuestionText: According to the module, for which type of data distribution is the Z-score method for outlier detection best suited?
Questions.Hint: Think about the assumption the Z-score method makes about the data.
Questions.Feedback: The Z-score method is best suited for normally distributed data. For skewed distributions, the IQR method is more robust. Choosing the right method depends on the underlying data distribution.

Options.Text: Normally distributed data
Right-skewed data
Bimodal data distributions
Categorical data with many unique values

Questions.Title: Outlier Retention in Fraud Detection
Questions.QuestionText: Why should outliers never be removed when building a fraud detection model?
Questions.Hint: Think about what role outliers play in fraud detection specifically.
Questions.Feedback: In fraud detection, outliers are precisely the records of interest — they represent the fraudulent transactions being sought. Removing them would eliminate the very signals the model needs to detect fraud.

Options.Text: Outliers represent the fraudulent transactions the model is designed to detect
Outliers always indicate data entry errors that would mislead the model
Removing outliers violates standard machine learning preprocessing rules
Outliers improve the statistical power of logistic regression models

Questions.Title: Winsorization as Outlier Treatment
Questions.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of Winsorization as an outlier treatment technique?
Questions.Hint: Think about retaining data points while limiting their influence.
Questions.Feedback: Winsorization retains the data point but limits its leverage on summary statistics by capping extreme values at a specified percentile, making it preferred when the analyst does not want to discard potentially valuable data.

Options.Text: To retain a data point while limiting its influence by capping extreme values
To permanently remove all values beyond three standard deviations
To replace outliers with the column mean or median value
To apply a logarithmic scale to normalize right-skewed distributions

Questions.Title: Example of a Conflicting Value
Questions.QuestionText: Which example best illustrates a conflicting value in a dataset?
Questions.Hint: Think about two fields in the same record containing logically contradictory information.
Questions.Feedback: Conflicting values occur when fields within the same record contradict each other, such as a 'Date of Birth' indicating a person is 10 years old while 'Employment Status' says 'Full-Time Employed'.

Options.Text: A record where Date of Birth indicates age 10 but Employment Status says Full-Time Employed
A phone number stored as '555-1234' in one record and '5551234' in another
A country field containing 'USA' and 'United States' in different rows
A dataset where income values are missing for 25% of respondents

Questions.Title: Standardizing Categorical Data Workflow
Questions.QuestionText: Which step is part of a common standardization workflow for categorical and text data?
Questions.Hint: Think about the specific text operations mentioned for making labels uniform.
Questions.Feedback: A common standardization workflow includes converting text to a uniform case (e.g., all lowercase), stripping leading/trailing whitespace, and mapping variant spellings to a canonical value using a lookup table.

Options.Text: Converting text to uniform case and stripping leading/trailing whitespace
Applying Z-score normalization to all string fields
Replacing all categorical values with numeric codes using random assignment
Dropping any column that contains more than two unique string values

Questions.Title: Purpose of a Data Cleaning Log
Questions.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of maintaining a data cleaning log during the cleaning process?
Questions.Hint: Think about reproducibility and accountability in data cleaning decisions.
Questions.Feedback: A data cleaning log ensures reproducibility and allows other team members or auditors to understand what was changed and why, creating an auditable trail essential for collaborative or regulated environments.

Options.Text: To ensure reproducibility and allow auditors to understand what was changed and why
To automatically revert datasets to their original state if errors are found
To store backup copies of the raw dataset before any transformations
To calculate summary statistics before and after cleaning operations

Questions.Title: Initial Dataset Assessment Tools
Questions.QuestionText: Which pandas methods are specifically mentioned as common starting points for an initial dataset assessment?
Questions.Hint: Think about the two pandas methods that provide summary statistics and structural information.
Questions.Feedback: Pandas .info() and .describe() are common starting points for initial dataset assessment, helping analysts quickly surface obvious problems such as incorrect data types or unexpected distributions.

Options.Text: .info() and .describe()
.duplicated() and .drop_duplicates()
.merge() and .groupby()
.replace() and .map()

Questions.Title: Automating Data Validation After Cleaning
Questions.QuestionText: Which libraries are mentioned as tools for automating data validation after cleaning?
Questions.Hint: Think about tools that allow teams to define and enforce data contracts.
Questions.Feedback: Great Expectations and pandera are mentioned as libraries that allow teams to define and enforce data contracts, automating validation checks each time new data is processed.

Options.Text: Great Expectations and pandera
NumPy and SciPy
Matplotlib and Seaborn
Scikit-learn and TensorFlow

Questions.Title: Data Lineage Tracking Importance
Questions.QuestionText: Why is data lineage tracking particularly important in regulated industries?
Questions.Hint: Think about what data lineage records and why transparency matters in regulated contexts.
Questions.Feedback: Data lineage tracking records where data originated, how it was transformed, and who made each change. This transparency is especially important in regulated industries where accountability and auditability are required.

Options.Text: It records data origins, transformations, and changes enabling accountability and auditability
It automatically corrects formatting errors found during data ingestion
It reduces the computational cost of processing large datasets
It prevents unauthorized users from accessing raw data files

Questions.Title: Final Preparation Step for Machine Learning
Questions.QuestionText: For machine learning pipelines, what specific formatting step is mentioned as part of final data preparation?
Questions.Hint: Think about how data must be divided before model training.
Questions.Feedback: For machine learning pipelines, final data preparation may include splitting data into training and test sets. For reporting, it may mean reshaping data into a wide or long format depending on the tool's requirements.

Options.Text: Splitting data into training and test sets
Applying log transformation to all numerical columns
Converting all categorical variables to binary dummy codes
Removing all columns with more than five unique values
